title: 第 46 節
en: the sun that gives light to men.' Agni was not only said to be the sun 1 ; some poets call him the son of the sun, and the sun the father of Agni 2. In one passage, Bv. I, 69, 1, the poet goes so far as to say, ' Being the son of the gods, thou hast become their father ' (Bhuva/i devanam pita putra/^ san). It is in this sense that later theologians maintain that the gods are itaretara-^anmana/^, as it were, 'mutually born.'

But though Agni was supposed to be everywhere, though in the Aitareya-brahmawa I, 1, 1, we meet with the well-known formula, Agnir vai sarva devaA, ' Agni is all deities, and all deities are Agni,' still a distinction is always maintained between Agni in his primary character as the house or family god, and Agni as in the sun, lighting the whole world, also as the dawn and the lightning. These latter characters were called by the Brahmans gau^a, secondary, the former mukhya or primary.

Fire in other Mythologies. In the other Aryan mythologies fire, as a purely elementary power, has never assumed the same prominence which it possessed in the Yeda and Avesta. The Greek mind had once for all accustomed itself to conceive all elementary powers under a human shape, and seemed incapable of conceiving anything divine except under that form. Instead of an Agni, as the fire on the hearth, which was Hestia as a female goddess, we should have expected therefore in Greece, if there were anything at all 1 Cf. Ehni, Yama, p. 66.
2 Agne/i surya^, suryad agnift. Nirukta, ed. Calc, vol. iii, p. 318.

Vl] FIRE IN OTHER MYTHOLOGIES. 791 corresponding to the Vedic Agni, some being in human shape connected with fire, or some more than human being who was believed to have discovered the fire, or to have brought it as a gift to man, by teaching them how to kindle it, how to keep it, and how to use it for practical purposes.

This change can be watched in its beginnings even in the Veda. Agni, besides being the fire, is also represented, though under a new name, as a workman using the fire for his own purposes. In that capacity, and generally under the name of Tvashtri, Agni is called su-apastama, the very clever workman, we might almost say the cunning smith. After that it required but a small step to bring us near to the Greek Hephaistos and the Italian Vulcan.
Hephaistos. Vulcan.

That both these gods were originally fire-gods was clear from the legends connected with them in Greek and Roman mythology. In the Iliad Hephaistos is sometimes used in the sense of fire, as when, in II. ii, 426 ; ix, 468, the Greeks hold the entrails to be roasted over Hephaistos. This may be called a mere case of metonymy or metaphor, as when the Romans used Ceres for bread, and Bacchus for wine.

A clearer trace, however, of the elementary background of Hephaistos can be recognised in his lameness. Why should Hephaistos be lame or bandy-legged ? In the Veda it is quite intelligible why Agni should be called apad, without feet, because there is nothing in the movements of Agni that could suggest the idea of feet. Fire advances in a meandering and serpentine manner. As there 792 HEPHAISTOS. VULCAN. [chap.

is not much difference between feetless and lame, the god without feet might well be changed into a lame god. Others, however, have seen the cause of the lameness of Hephaistos in the crooked descent of the lightning, and if we consider that the Greeks accounted for the god's lameness by his mother having hurled him from the sky, or by his father having; thrown him down to the isle of Lemnos, much might be said for the identification of the lame god with the lightning rushing tortuously from the clouds to the earth. Only it has never been proved that the Greek Hephaistos was meant originally for the fire of lightning, though in the Veda Agni, no doubt, having assumed the general character of light, wherever perceived, was recognised in the fire of lightning, as well as in all other manifestations of fire. Lemnos being volcanic, it is easy to understand why Hephaistos should have been represented as banished from the Olympian gods, and dwelling in the dark caverns of the island. All these elementary thoughts, at first independent of one another, would afterwards be woven together, as we find them in Greek mythology. There was no difficulty in finding a reason why Hephaistos, the lame god, dwelt at Lemnos, and why Zeus and Here, originally conceived as his parents, were angry with him. If at any time he had taken the part of his mother against his father, what was more natural than that Zeus should be represented as punishing or actually disowning his son ? Then another story had to be invented of his mother having given birth to him by herself, just as Zeus was supposed to have brought forth Athene by himself. All this was done without much difficulty, for we must always remember that Vl] HEPHAISTOS. VULCAN. 793 there was no one to contradict the story-teller or story-singer who travelled from village to village to amuse the people. The story of Athene being the child of Zeus alone and springing from the head of Zeus, had no doubt an independent origin and a physical background ; the story of Here giving birth to Hephaistos looks more like a later addition, merely suggested by the presupposed jealousy of Here, and her wish to give her husband tit for tat.

We must not forget, however, that Hephaistos has himself also an active part to perform at the miraculous birth of Athene. It is he who splits the forehead of Zeus. This forehead of Zeus, the murdha Diva/i, is meant for the East \ and if Athene was meant for the light of the dawn, Hephaistos must on this occasion have been conceived in the same character in which we see him in the Veda as Agni, viz. the light of the sun, opening every morning the gates of heaven, another name for the forehead of Zeus. We saw that the gates of heaven were meant for the East. Scholars who take Athene as representing the lightning, would find it difficult to account for the lightning (Hephaistos) assisting at the birth of lightning (Athene), or for the forehead or the head of Zeus being the birthplace of lightning, considering that lightning is seen to spring from every part of the sky, and not from the East only. Lightning is in fact too sudden, too transitory a phenomenon to be easily changed into a permanent, or everlasting deity. If, on the contrary, we take Athene or Ahana for the Dawn, the Sun-maiden, 1 Agni himself is sometimes called murdha, Diva/j, cf. Kv. VI, 794 HEPHAISTOS. VULCAN. [chap.

springing from the forehead of the sky, Hephaistos would then be the personified morning light, distinguished from the feminine Dawn by his masculine name, an Agnir aushasa, a kind of Pater matutinus or Janus. We should then understand also why this son of the morning, this Hephaistos, was represented as in love with the Dawn, and why Athene, like Daphne, should have been fabled to have fled from his amorous advances. In another shape, and under another name, it is really the Dawn that becomes in the end the wife of Hephaistos. In the Iliad the wife of Hephaistos is still called Charis, in the Theogony Aglaia, the brilliant, while in the Odyssey she is Aphrodite herself, all, as can easily be seen, originally names of the Dawn.

When occupying his Olympian abode, Hephaistos is taken as the workman of the gods, the clever smith or artist. The chariot of Helios, the armour of Herakles, of Diomedes, of Achilles, the sceptre of Zeus and his golden throne, all are his handiwork. The shield of Achilles, as described in the Iliad, shows how far art had been carried in the smithies of Homer's time. His Vedic representative, Tvash£?^ (reKToov), is likewise fabled to have forged the thunderbolt of Indra (Kv. I, 32, 2), and to have sharpened the axe of Brahma?iaspati (X, 53, 9). He is conceived as the workman of all things, as having made the various forms of all living beings (Rv. 1, 188, 9), and finally as having created the whole world ( Vag. S. XXIX, 9). He is called Visvarupa, i.e. possessing all forms, though after a time Visvarupa (often supposed to be a name of the moon) is represented as his son (Tvashfra). There is very little of fixed relationship, as yet, as we saw, among the gods of Vl] HEPHAISTOS. VULCAN. 795 the Veda, and we must not be surprised, therefore, if we see this Tvash^H, that is, Agni, spoken of in other places as the father of Agni (Rv. X, 46, 9), nay as Dyaus himself.

So much for the general concej^tion of Hephaistos among the Greeks. We saw that his worship in Lemnos may have been due to two causes, partly to the volcanic character of the island, partly to the presence of forges worked in the town, which was called Hephaistias, after the god, and elsewhere in the island. In this local character Hephaistos has become the smith, the cunning artist, the sorcerer, slightly deformed and grimy, yet strong and powerful in his limbs. The character of those whose trade he protected was naturally reflected on him, but in spite of these somewhat homely features, we must never forget that Hephaistos, as the god of fire, was originally one of the great Olympian deities, the child of Zeus and Here, and that the most beautiful of the Greek goddesses was, for some sufficient reason, given him as his wife.

It is clear even from these few coincidences between Vedic and Greek mythology that it would be a mistake to expect a whole cluster of names and myths connected with fire to be the same in India and in Greece. As in comparing the two languages we find but few compounds, still fewer phrases, the same in Greek and Sanskrit, such as evecrrw = sv-asti, ha>Tr}pe<s idcov = data vasunam, ratam astu = ratum-esto 1 ; we must not expect any more to find composite stories of gods the same in the Veda and in Homer. What we find are the floating atoms, the mythological 1 K. Z., viii, p. 64.
VOL. II. B b 796 HEPHAISTOS. VULCAN. [CHAP.

roots or germs, growing up independently each on its own soil, so that in their later growth, as we watch it in the mythologies known to us, they are often changed beyond all recognition. A kaleidoscope well shaken seems to give the best idea of the evervarying aspects of ancient Aryan mythology. While Agni in India gave rise to an abundant growth of mythology, we find ignis in Latin perfectly sterile mythologically. We find Agni represented as TvashtfW in the Veda, the clever workman, in Greek K\vTOTe-)(vr}<;, TrokvfjLrjTLs, xaAKeu<?, the smith, but likewise the maker or creator of all things, while in the Greek Sagas Hephaistos became a well-marked legendary figure, involved in many more or less creditable adventures, but never, even in his few local sanctuaries, rising to the rank of creator and ruler of the world.
Bhurawyu = Phordneus.

We have now to look at another word, which in the Veda is applied to Agni, and wThich has left some traces in Greek. Bhura^yu is an epithet of Agni in the Veda, and is, as I showed in the chapter on Phonetics, the same word as Phoroneus in Greek. It is derived from bhur, to move quickly; and, as applied to the fire, it means moving, glittering, flickering, blazing. Bhur may be a secondary form of bhar, if bhar ever had the meaning of vah, to carry, from which we have vehemens, carried along, and Sk. vahni, fire, as the name of Agni has been connected with ag-ilis in the sense of moving quickly. Bhuranyati occurs in the sense of bubbling, stirring, and bhura^yu would be a regular derivative of that verb. The Greek Qopcovevs, if not exactly the same Vl] BHURAJVYU = PHORONEUS. 797 word, is clearly derived from the same root \ and this is all we have any right to expect in mythological names. Phoroneus, however, in Greek mythology is no longer the name of a god, least of all of the god of fire. It is the name of a mythical king in the Peloponnesos, who must have occupied an important place in ancient Greek mythology, but of whom we do not know much more than that he induced the people now scattered in Argos to live together, that he founded a town, and that he instituted sacrifices to Here. If, as Pausanias tells us, he was also supposed to have been the discoverer of fire, we can hardly hesitate to recognise in him a representative of the fire, a kind of Hephaistos or Prometheus, only lost in local legends and probably little known in later times beyond the confines of Argos. He is connected, however, with the general mythology of Greece by being represented as the father both of to and of Niobe. There are traces also of his having once been considered as the father of all mankind 2, and a famous poem is mentioned, the Phoronis, which would have told us more of what the Greeks believed about him. His mother, Melia, the ash-tree, may likewise contain a recollection of the fire, in very ancient times, having been obtained from the wood of that tree by friction, just as Agni is said to have been born alive from dry wood, Kv.

1 Certain phonetic difficulties have been fully discussed by Kuhn (Herabkunft des Feuers, p. 27) ; and need not be discussed here again. Pott re-opened the question in Kuhn's Zeitschrift, ix, p. 342, but left it much as it was. We know now that there was even in Sanskrit a form bharawyu by the side of bhuranyu, which removes all difficulties.
2 Plato, Timaeus, p. 22.
B b 2 798 BHUKAA'YU = PHORONEUS. [chap.

I, 68, 2 : $ushkat yat deva giv&h g&mshth&h. If fire was represented as the first of men, or as the half-divine ancestor of families and clans, the firewood (Melia) l would naturally claim the place of the first mother, the ancestress of mankind.
His father Inachos was evidently meant for the river in Argos, on the shores of which Phoroneus established his settlements.

It might seem as if we did not gain very much by being able to connect Phoroneus with Bhuranyu, and by discovering in Bhurar/yu a name of fire and its original meaning of vehement. And yet these are very important discoveries as connected with the general problem of mythology. Instead of leaving us with the bare statement that Phoroneus was the son of Inachos (river) and of Melia (ash-tree), we can now recognise in him one of the many names of fire ; we see in fact a god changed as it were before our eyes into a local hero, and we learn also that fire was recognised as the sine qua non of social and civilised life. Fire was the first step in civilisation, and in the hymns of the Bigveda the enemies of the Aryas are spoken of as without fire, or as not keeping the fire (anagnitraA) 2 . It was from this point of view that Phoroneus could be looked upon as the founder of towns (acrrv (f>opa)fi/<oV),and as the ancestor of the royal family of Argos, just as Agni in the Veda was conceived as Vrs-pati, the lord of clans, and as the ancestor of various families. In this way both his father and mother 1 Kuhn takes Melia, to be one of the Melian nymphs, originally a cloud holding the fire of lightning.
2 Rv. I, 189, 1-4; Winternitz, Sarpabali, p. 25; M. M., Letter on Turanian Languages, p. 85.

Vl] BHUKAATYU = PHORONEUS. 799 become intelligible, and instead of an unmeaning myth, we get simple and natural ideas of the ancient world expressed in language that was equally simple and natural so long as it was rightly understood. Lastly, unless we chose to look on two such words as Bhuran-yu and Phoroneus as the result of mere accident, or of two independent acts, we gain the conviction that before their final separation, the Aryan sjDeakers possessed a common fund of mythological names, and thus learn once more the lesson that if on one side we must not look for an elementary deity of fire in Greece, we ought not on the other to try to discover either the river Inachos or the tree Melia in India.
Vulcanus. Ulk&.

Let us now look back once more to Vulcanus and Hephaistos. We found no god Ignis in Latin, corresponding to the Yedic Agni, nor do we find in the Veda a god having the name of Vulcanus. Still what we find, little as it may seem, is of some importance. The formation of the name Vulcanus is thoroughly Latin, nus being a Latin derivative. And in the base Vulca even so early a student of Sanskrit as Schlegel easily discovered the Sanskrit Ulka, a firebrand, a flame, a word which, as I showed in Kuhh's Zeitschrift of 1869, really occurs in the Veda under a slightly different form. There we read, Bv. IV, 4, 2 : Asandita/^ vi sriga, vishvak ulkaA, ' Being unrestrained, 0 Agni, send out thy sparks in all directions.' This ul in ulka is, however, a contraction only of val or var, and it was Grassmann who in 1867 (K. Z., xvi, p. 164) pointed out a number of passages in the Big-veda in which 800 VULCANUS. ULKA. [chap.

var&as occurs in the sense of fire or light. Rv. Ill, 22, 2 : Agne yat te divi var&as prithivyam yat dshadhishu apsii a ya^atra, ' 0 Agni, what light of thine is in heaven, on earth, in the plants, and in the waters ! '
zh: 照亮人類的太陽。』阿格尼（Agni）不僅被說是太陽¹；有些詩人稱他為太陽之子，又稱太陽為阿格尼的父親²。在一處經文中，《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第六十九讚第一節，詩人甚至說：『作為諸神之子，你已成為他們的父親』（Bhuvá devanam pitá putro san）。正是在這個意義上，後世的神學家主張諸神乃是互生相成的（itaretara-janmana），如同彼此誕生。

但雖然阿格尼（Agni）被認為無所不在，雖然在《愛達羅耶梵書》（Aitareya-brahmawa）I, 1, 1 中，我們遇到了那個著名的公式——「阿格尼誠為諸提婆，諸提婆亦為阿格尼」（Agnir vai sarva devaA）——然而古代婆羅門人始終保持著一個區分，即阿格尼在其家庭神或族神的原初性質與阿格尼作為太陽（照亮整個世界）、作為晨曦與閃電的身份之間的區分。後者被婆羅門人稱為「次要的」（gau^a），前者稱為「本質的」或「原初的」（mukhya or primary）。
在其他雅利安神話中，火作為純粹的元素力量，從未如同在吠陀與阿維斯陀中那樣獲得同等的重要性。希臘人的思想早已習慣於將所有元素力量概念化為人類的形態，似乎無法在此形式之外想像任何神聖的事物。因此，在希臘，我們應當預期有一種對應於阿格尼（Agni）的神祇——作為爐火的神，在希臘就是女神赫斯提亞（Hestia）——倘若當時確有此物的話¹ ¹ 參見《埃尼、亞瑪》，第66頁。
2 Agne/i surya^, suryad agnift. 《尼魯克塔》(Nirukta)，加爾各答版，第三卷，第318頁。
火在其他神話中的對應。791 與吠陀的阿耆尼（Agni）相應，其中有些呈現人形，與火相聯繫，或者是某個超越人性的存在，人們相信他發現了火，或者作為禮物將火帶給人類，並教導他們如何點燃火焰、如何保持火焰，以及如何將火用於實際目的。

這種變化甚至可以在《吠陀》的初期階段被觀察到。阿耆尼（Agni）除了是火之外，還以一個新的名字被代表，是一個為了自己的目的而使用火的工匠。在這種身分下，以及通常以特瓦什特里（Tvashtri）之名，阿耆尼被稱為蘇-阿帕斯塔瑪（su-apastama），即非常聰慧的工匠，我們幾乎可以說是狡詐的鍛工。此後，只需要一小步，就能使我們接近希臘的赫菲斯托斯（Hephaistos）和義大利的烏爾甘（Vulcan）。
赫淮斯托斯。火神。
這兩位神祇原本都是火神，這點從希臘與羅馬神話中與它們相關的傳說可以清楚看出。在《伊利亞德》中，赫淮斯托斯（Hephaistos）有時被用來表示火，例如在卷二第 426 行和第 468 行中，希臘人將內臟放在赫淮斯托斯上方烤炙。這可以稱為純粹的換喻或隱喻之例，就如同羅馬人用刻瑞斯（Ceres）代表麵包，用巴克斯（Bacchus）代表葡萄酒一樣。
然而，在赫菲斯托斯的跛足特徵中，可以更清晰地追溯到其基本背景。為何赫菲斯托斯應當是跛足或羅圈腿的呢？在《吠陀》中，阿格尼被稱為「無足者」（apad）就相當容易理解，因為在阿格尼的運動方式中，沒有任何事物能暗示足的觀念。火的運動方式是蜿蜒迂迴且蛇形的。由於

無腳與跛足之間並無太大差別，無腳的神明自可轉化為跛足之神。然而，另有人士認為赫淮斯托斯（Hephaistos）跛足的原因在於閃電下降之彎曲路徑。若考慮到希臘人以他母親將他從天空擲下，或他父親將他投入雷姆諾斯島來解釋該神之跛足，則將跛足之神與自雲中蜿蜒奔向大地之閃電相等同，實有其可言之處。然而從未有人證明希臘的赫淮斯托斯原本是指閃電之火，雖然在吠陀中，阿耆尼（Agni）無疑已具有普遍光之性質，故在閃電之火及一切其他火之顯現中皆被認出。既然雷姆諾斯為火山島，則易於理解赫淮斯托斯何以被描繪為遭奧林帕斯諸神放逐，而居住於該島黑暗洞窟之中。凡此種種最初相互獨立之基本思想，其後遂編織為一體，正如我們在希臘神話中所見者。尋求赫淮斯托斯此跛足之神何以居住雷姆諾斯，以及最初被視為其父母之宙斯與赫拉何以對他不滿之理由，並無困難。若他曾擁護其母反抗其父，宙斯以懲罰或實際上遺棄其子相報，更何足奇？隨後又必須編造另一故事，謂其母獨自生下了他，正如宙斯被認為獨自生出雅典娜（Athene）一樣。凡此種種之為，無甚難處，蓋我輩必須時刻銘記，沒有任何人足以駁斥那往返村里間藉以娛樂民眾之故事述說者或故事歌者。雅典娜為宙斯獨生之子，自宙斯頭部迸出之故事，無疑具有獨立之起源與物質背景；赫拉生下赫淮斯托斯之故事毋寧較似於後起之附加，不過係由預設之赫拉的妒忌與以牙還牙之願望所暗示罷了。

我們絕不可忘記，赫菲斯托斯（Hephaistos）本身在雅典娜（Athene）的奇蹟誕生中也扮演著一個積極的角色。正是他劈開了宙斯的額頭。這個宙斯的額頭——提婆的頭頂（murdha Diva/i）——是針對東方而言的；而如果雅典娜代表曙光，那麼赫菲斯托斯在這個場合必定是以我們在吠陀中所見到的阿耆尼（Agni）的同一性質而被構想的，即太陽之光，每個早晨打開天堂之門，這是宙斯額頭的另一個名稱。我們已經看到，天堂之門是針對東方而言的。那些將雅典娜視為代表閃電的學者，會發現很難解釋為什麼閃電（赫菲斯托斯）協助閃電（雅典娜）的誕生，或為什麼宙斯的額頭或頭部是閃電的誕生地，因為閃電事實上是從天空的各個部分迸發，而非僅從東方迸發。閃電實在太突然、太短暫的現象，不易被轉變成一位永恆的、或永遠的神祇。相反地，如果我們將雅典娜或阿哈那（Ahana）視為曙光、太陽之女神，¹ 則阿耆尼本身有時也被稱為頭頂（murdha, Diva/j，參閱梨俱吠陀第六卷第794頌）赫菲斯托斯。烏爾坎（HEPHAISTOS. VULCAN）。〔章

從天空的額頭迸發而出，赫淮斯托斯（Hephaistos）便是擬人化的晨光，藉其陽性名字而有別於陰性的曙光女神；一如阿格尼與晨曦（Agnir aushasa）、一種晨父（Pater matutinus）或雅努斯（Janus）。我們於是便能理解，為何這位晨光之子、這位赫淮斯托斯，被描繪為戀慕曙光女神，以及為何雅典娜如同達芙妮（Daphne）一般，傳說中應當逃離他的愛慕糾纏。在另一種形態之下、以另一個名字呈現，曙光女神實際上最終確實成為赫淮斯托斯的妻子。在《伊利亞德》中，赫淮斯托斯的妻子仍被稱為卡麗絲（Charis），在《神統記》中稱為光燦的阿格萊亞（Aglaia），而在《奧德賽》中她就是阿佛洛狄忒本身——這些名字，如不難看出，最初都是曙光女神的稱號。

當赫菲斯托斯（Hephaistos）占據其奧林匹斯的住所時，他被視為眾神的工匠，靈巧的鐵匠或藝術家。赫利俄斯（Helios）的戰車、赫拉克勒斯（Herakles）、迪奧墨得斯（Diomedes）和阿基琉斯（Achilles）的盔甲、宙斯（Zeus）的權杖及其黃金王座，這一切都是他的傑作。如《伊利亞德》中所描述的阿基琉斯之盾，展現了荷馬時代鍛造工坊的藝術水準之高。他在吠陀中的對應人物特瓦什塔（Tvash£?^；τέκτων），同樣被傳說為曾鍛造因陀羅（Indra）的雷霆（Kv. I, 32, 2），並磨利了梵天師（Brahma?iaspati）的斧頭（X, 53, 9）。他被構想為萬物的工匠，被謂為製造了所有生物的各種形態（Rv. 1, 188, 9），最後更被視為創造了整個世界的存在（Vag. S. XXIX, 9）。他被稱為毗濕瓦盧波（Visvarupa），即具有一切形態者，不過後來毗濕瓦盧波（常被認為是月亮的名字）被描繪為他的兒子（特瓦什塔之子）。正如我們所見，在吠陀的諸神中，固定的關係甚少，因此我們不應驚訝，這位特瓦什塔，也就是阿格尼（Agni），在其他地方被言及為阿格尼的父親（Rv. X, 46, 9），甚至被言及為特尤斯（Dyaus）本身。

關於赫菲斯托斯在希臘人中的普遍觀念，我們的論述至此為止。我們看到他在勒姆諾斯島的信仰可能源於兩個原因：一方面是該島的火山特性，另一方面是島上鍛造坊的存在，該坊所在的城鎮以神名「赫菲斯提亞斯」（Hephaistias）命名，島上其他地方亦有類似的鍛造場所。在這種地方性特徵中，赫菲斯托斯逐漸演變為鐵匠、巧妙的藝術家、魔法師——身形略顯殘缺，滿身煤垢，然而四肢強健有力。那些受他庇護的工匠的性格特徵自然而然地投映在他身上。然而，儘管具有這些頗為平凡的特質，我們決不能忘記：赫菲斯托斯作為火神，原本乃是奧林匹斯山上的偉大神祇之一，是宙斯和赫拉的兒子，而希臘眾神中最為美麗的女神，為了某種充分的理由，被許配給了他作為妻子。

從吠陀與希臘神話之間這幾個巧合之處，已可明確看出，若期待火焰相關的整套名字與神話在印度與希臘完全相同，那將是一個錯誤。正如在比較兩種語言時，我們發現只有少數複合詞、更少的短語在希臘語與梵語之間相同，例如 ἔχω = sva-asti、δαίτης ἰδέων = data vasunam、ratam astu = ratum-esto¹；同樣地，我們也不應期待在吠陀與荷馬作品中發現相同的複合神祇故事。我們所發現的是浮游的原子，神話學上的¹ ¹ K. Z., viii, p. 64.
**赫淮斯托斯‧瓦爾坎** 赫淮斯托斯（Hephaistos）與瓦爾坎（Vulcan）

根或胚芽，各自在其獨立的土壤上生長，因此在其後來的發展中，正如我們在已知的神話學中所觀察到的，它們往往被改變到完全認不出來的地步。一個被充分搖晃的萬花筒似乎最能說明古代雅利安神話學的千變萬化之相。當印度的阿格尼（Agni）衍生出豐富的神話增長時，我們在拉丁文中發現伊格尼斯（ignis）在神話上完全是貧瘠的。我們發現阿格尼在吠陀中被表現為特瓦什特里（Tvashtr），即聰慧的工匠，在希臘文中為工匠凱克托斯（κεκτοχνης）、波俄克美蒂斯（ποκημητις）、卡爾克烏斯（χαλκευς），同時也是萬物的製造者或創造者，而在希臘傳說中，赫菲斯托斯（Hephaistos）成了一個界線分明的傳奇人物，涉及許多或多或少聲名狼藉的冒險，但即使在他為數不多的地方聖所中，也從未上升到世界的創造者和統治者的地位。
婆羅瓦由 = 弗耳德涅烏斯。

我們現在必須考察另一個詞彙，它在《吠陀》中被用以稱呼阿格尼（Agni），並在希臘語中留下了某些痕跡。在《吠陀》中，布拉儒（Bhura^yu）是阿格尼的一個上稱，正如我在《音韻學》章節所示，它與希臘語中的福羅涅烏斯（Phoroneus）是同一個詞。它源自「布爾」（bhur），意為迅速移動；當應用於火時，它意指移動、閃耀、搖曳、熊熊燃燒。布爾可能是「巴爾」（bhar）的次生形式，如果巴爾曾經具有「瓦赫」（vah）的意義，亦即運載，由此我們有「韋赫門斯」（vehemens，被運送的）與梵文「瓦赫尼」（vahni，火），阿格尼的名稱曾被與「阿格─伊利斯」（ag-ilis）相連結，其義為迅速移動。「布拉儒耶蒂」（Bhuranyati）出現於冒泡、攪動的意義中，而「布拉儒」（bhura^yu）將是該動詞的規則衍生物。希臘語的「福羅涅烏斯」（Qopcovevs）若非確實相同的詞彙，也明顯地源自同一根源，而這正是我們在神話學名稱中有權期待的全部。然而，福羅涅烏斯在希臘神話中不再是一位神祇的名稱，更不用說是火神了。它是伯羅奔尼撒的一位神話國王的名稱，他必然在古代希臘神話中佔據重要地位，但我們對他知之甚少，只知他誘使現今散居於阿哥斯的民眾聚居一處，他建立了一座城鎮，並制定了向赫拉（Here）獻祭的儀式。若如鮑桑尼亞斯（Pausanias）所告訴我們的，他也曾被認為是火的發現者，我們幾乎不能不在他身上認出火的代表者，一種赫菲斯托斯（Hephaistos）或普羅米修斯（Prometheus）式的人物，只不過陷入了地方性的傳說中，且在後世時代可能在阿哥斯的邊界之外鮮為人知。然而，他被呈現為女神伊俄（Io）與尼俄柏（Niobe）的父親，而與希臘的普遍神話相連結。也有跡象表明他曾一度被視為全人類的父親，而一部著名的詩篇《福羅尼斯》（Phoronis）被提及，該詩本會告訴我們更多有關希臘人對他的信念。他的母親梅利亞（Melia），即白蠟樹，同樣可能包含著對火的一種追憶，在非常古老的時代，人們曾以摩擦白蠟樹的木料而取得火焰，正如據說阿格尼曾從乾木中活生生地誕生（梨俱吠陀）。
某些語音困難已經由庫恩充分討論過（《火的起源》第27頁）；在此無須再次討論。波特在庫恩的《雜誌》第九卷第342頁重新提出這個問題，但基本上並未有所改進。我們現在知道在梵語中甚至存在著 bharawyu 這樣的形式，與 bhuranyu 並行使用，這消除了所有的困難。
柏拉圖，《蒂邁歐篇》，第 22 頁。
B b 2 798 BHUKAA'YU = PHORONEUS. [第
I, 68, 2：「烏什卡特 亞特 提婆 吉瓦赫 葛姆舒塔赫」(ushkat yat deva giv&h g&mshth&h)。倘若火被表現為人類中的第一個，或作為家族與氏族中半神聖的祖先，則薪木（梅利亞）(Melia)必然理應佔據第一位母親、人類女始祖的地位。
她的父親伊那科斯（Inachos）顯然是指阿爾戈斯的河流，福羅涅烏斯就在這條河的岸邊建立了他的居地。

看起來，將 Phoroneus 與 Bhuranyu 連繫起來，並在 Bhuranyu 中發現火的名稱及其「劇烈」的本義，似乎所得甚微。然而，這些發現作為神話學一般問題的相關研究，實具極大重要性。我們不再只能陳述 Phoroneus 乃 Inachos（河流）與 Melia（白蠟樹）之子這一赤裸的說法，而是可以在他身上認出眾多火的名稱之一；我們事實上看到一位神祇彷彿在我們眼前化身為地方英雄，並且我們也得知火被認為是社會文明生活的絕對必要條件。火乃文明化的第一步，在《梨俱吠陀》的讚歌中，雅利安人的敵人被述及為無火的，或不守火者（anagnitraA）。正是從這一觀點出發，Phoroneus 才可被視為城鎮的建立者（ἀστυ φορανομός），以及阿哥斯王族的祖先，就如同吠陀中的 Agni 被構想為 Vrs-pati——氏族之主，以及各個家族的祖先。由此方式，他的父親與母親皆得以解釋——
2 梨俱吠陀 I, 189, 1-4；溫特尼茨，《蛇祭》，第25頁；穆勒，《圖蘭語族書信》，第85頁。

VI] BHUKAATYU = PHORONEUS. 799 變得可以理解，我們不再面對毫無意義的神話，而是得到古代世界簡樸自然的觀念，這些觀念以同樣簡樸自然的語言表達，只要我們正確地理解它。最後，除非我們願意把 Bhuran-yu 和 Phoroneus 這樣的兩個詞視為純粹偶然或各自獨立的行為的結果，否則我們會確信在最終分離之前，雅利安語族使用者擁有共同的神話名稱庫，由此再次得到教訓：如果一方面我們不應該在希臘尋找火的基本神祇，那麼另一方面，我們也不應該試圖在印度發現伊那科斯河（Inachos）或梅利亞樹（Melia）。

I notice the English text provided appears to be incomplete or fragmentary: "Vulcanus. Ulk&." This seems to be just a name (Vulcanus, the Roman god of fire and metalworking) followed by what looks like an incomplete entry or typographical error ("Ulk&"). Without a complete English passage to translate, I cannot provide a proper translation. Could you please provide the full English text passage from Müller's 1873 *Introduction to the Science of Religion* that you'd like me to translate?

讓我們再次回顧特尤斯和赫菲斯托斯的情形。我們在拉丁文中找不到與吠陀的阿格尼（Agni）相應的火神伊格尼斯（Ignis），同樣在吠陀中也找不到名叫沃爾卡努斯的提婆。儘管如此，我們所發現的，雖然看起來微不足道，卻具有某種重要性。沃爾卡努斯（Vulcanus）這個名字的構成方式純粹是拉丁文的，其中 nus 是拉丁文的派生詞尾。而在詞根 Vulca 中，連早期的梵文學者施萊格爾（Schlegel）都輕易地發現了梵文詞彙烏爾卡（Ulka），意為火把、火焰，這個詞如我在 1869 年《庫恩時報》（Kuhh's Zeitschrift）中所示的，確實以稍異的形式出現在吠陀中。我們在那裡讀到《梨俱吠陀》第四卷第 4 節第 2 句：Asandita vi sriga, vishvak ulka，意為「不受羈絆之物啊，阿格尼，請向四面八方發出你的火星。」然而，這個出現在 ulka 中的 ul 實際上只是 val 或 var 的縮略形式。正是格拉斯曼在 1867 年（《比較語言學時報》第十六卷第 164 頁）指出了梨俱吠陀中許多段落，其中
梵文 var&as 具有火或光的含義。《梨俱吠陀》第三卷，22，2：Agne yat te divi var&as prithivyam yat dshadhishu apsii a ya^atra，「噢阿耆尼，你的光芒存在於天、地、植物和水中！」
